overview.global_summary: Across 32 chunks spanning at least two distinct time periods (2005-12-05 and 2015-07-11 through 2015-12-10), three major incident threads emerge. (1) Apache/mod_jk instability (chunks 1–2, 7–8): A persistent mod_jk connector failure cycle dominates the 2005-12-05 window, with Apache continuously spawning and crashing worker children. Concurrently, five distinct external IPs probe the web root for directory listings, suggesting opportunistic reconnaissance against a degraded server. (2) Multi-vector external brute-force campaign (chunks 14, 16–18, 20, 22–24): The 2015-07 through 2015-12 window is dominated by sustained, automated SSH brute-force from numerous global IPs targeting root and common service accounts, combined with high-rate FTP connection floods from separate IPs. Chunks 17–18 show very high authz_security counts (195 each) with accompanying network activity, and chunks 20 and 23 contain the most detailed evidence of coordinated multi-source SSH attacks. One suspicious successful SSH login (user fztu, uid=0, from 119.137.62.142) occurs in chunk 20 amid active brute-force activity. (3) Recurring process/system-configuration baseline (chunks 25–32): A stable, repeating pattern of process lifecycle and system configuration events with modest network activity suggests normal scheduled operations (cron, service restarts), but the consistency across 8 chunks warrants baselining to distinguish noise from anomaly. Logrotate failures recur daily throughout the 2015 window, indicating a persistent misconfiguration. The overall posture is HIGH risk: active credential-attack campaigns, a potentially compromised account (fztu), a degraded application stack, and unresolved configuration drift.

top_correlations.explanation: High-rate SSH brute-force events (chunks 14, 17, 18, 20, 22, 23) and FTP flood events (chunks 14, 16, 17) overlap in the 2015-07 to 2015-12-10 window. The co-occurrence of both attack vectors on the same host within the same time window, from different source IPs, is consistent with a shared botnet or coordinated campaign scanning multiple services simultaneously.

A successful SSH login for user fztu (uid=0) from 119.137.62.142 occurs at 09:32:20 on 2015-12-10 (chunk 20), exactly 15 minutes after the start of a multi-source SSH brute-force campaign. The session lasts 13 minutes and involves a root-equivalent account. This temporal proximity and privilege level strongly suggest the login may be the result of a successful brute-force or credential-reuse attack.

The mod_jk workerEnv error cycle in the 2005-12-05 window (chunks 1–2, 7–8) repeats 60+ times across a ~5.5-hour window. The pattern of jk2_init → workerEnv.init OK → workerEnv error state, combined with continuously incrementing PIDs, confirms a crash-restart loop. This is a systemic application-layer failure, not a transient event.

Daily logrotate failures (exit code 1) throughout the 2015-07 window (chunk 14) combined with a cupsd+syslogd restart on 2015-07-17 during the logrotate window raise the possibility that log rotation is not completing successfully. If logs are not rotating, disk space exhaustion or log truncation could suppress evidence of ongoing attacks, creating a blind spot during the active brute-force campaign.

Five distinct external IPs probe /var/www/html/ for directory listings during the same 5.5-hour window as the mod_jk crash loop (chunk 8, 2005-12-05). The degraded application state may have made the server more visible or accessible to scanners, or the reconnaissance may have been triggered by the server's abnormal response patterns.

hypotheses: H1 — Coordinated SSH+FTP credential-stuffing campaign (2015): The simultaneous appearance of high-rate SSH brute-force (chunks 14, 17, 18, 20, 22, 23) and FTP connection floods (chunks 14, 16, 17) from geographically diverse IPs across the same multi-day window suggests a single coordinated campaign or shared attack toolkit/botnet targeting both SSH (port 22) and FTP (port 21) on the same host.

H2 — Successful SSH compromise via brute-force (chunk 20): The accepted password login for user 'fztu' (uid=0) from 119.137.62.142 at 09:32:20 on 2015-12-10, occurring 15 minutes into an active multi-source brute-force campaign, may represent a successful intrusion. The 13-minute interactive session and root-equivalent UID strongly suggest post-exploitation activity.

H3 — Apache/mod_jk misconfiguration enabling reconnaissance (2005, chunks 1–2, 7–8): The persistent mod_jk workerEnv error cycle (60+ occurrences) degraded the web application stack, and the concurrent directory-listing probes from 5 distinct IPs may indicate that attackers detected the degraded state and escalated enumeration. The instability may have been triggered or exploited by the reconnaissance activity.

H4 — Logrotate misconfiguration causing service disruption (2015, chunk 14): Daily logrotate exit-code-1 failures across the entire 2015-07 window, combined with a cupsd+syslogd restart on 2015-07-17 during the logrotate window, suggest a broken post-rotate script that is intermittently disrupting services. This could suppress log evidence of attacks if log files are not rotating correctly.

H5 — Chunks 25–32 represent a stable operational baseline: The highly consistent process_lifecycle (~45/chunk) and system_configuration (~8–12/chunk) counts across 8 consecutive chunks with near-zero security events suggest a scheduled-job or service-heartbeat baseline. Any future deviation from these counts in these categories should be treated as an anomaly indicator.

H6 — Multi-epoch log aggregation: The presence of events from both 2005 and 2015 in the same dataset suggests logs from multiple systems or time periods have been aggregated. Correlations across epochs should be treated as independent incident threads unless a shared infrastructure link is confirmed.
timeline_highlights: 2005-12-05T13:53:35Z — Apache mod_jk workerEnv enters persistent error state; crash-restart cycle begins (chunk 8, CORR-03)
2005-12-05T14:11:43Z — First of 5 distinct external IPs probes /var/www/html/ directory listing (141.154.18.244); distributed web root reconnaissance begins (chunk 8, CORR-04)
2005-12-05T16:45:04Z–19:14:09Z — Four additional IPs (216.216.185.130, 218.75.106.250, 68.228.3.15, 61.220.139.68) probe web root across ~2.5-hour window; mod_jk crash loop still active (chunk 8)
2005-12-05T19:15:00Z — Approximate end of observed mod_jk crash-restart cycle (chunk 8)
2015-07-11T17:58:21Z — First SSH root brute-force attempt observed (211.137.205.253); marks start of multi-week credential attack campaign (chunk 14)
2015-07-12T04:03:42Z — First logrotate abnormal exit (code 1); recurs daily through at least 2015-07-17 (chunk 14, CORR-05)
2015-07-12T06:09:43Z — 9 rapid SSH root failures from 68.143.156.89 in 3 seconds (chunk 14)
2015-07-13T17:22:28Z — Suspicious SSH session for generic 'test' account opened/closed 3 times in 1 second (chunk 14)
2015-07-15T23:42:43Z — 22 FTP connections from 211.107.232.1 in 1 second; FTP flood campaign begins (chunk 14, CORR-01)
2015-07-17T04:06:32Z — 30+ FTP connections from 83.116.207.11 across two bursts; cupsd+syslogd restart during logrotate window (chunk 14)
2015-12-10T09:17:26Z — Multi-source SSH brute-force campaign begins; 187.141.143.180 cycles 24+ usernames at ~5s intervals (chunk 20, CORR-07)
2015-12-10T09:32:20Z — CRITICAL: Successful SSH login for fztu (uid=0) from 119.137.62.142 amid active brute-force; 13-minute interactive session (chunk 20, CORR-02)
2015-12-10T09:45:06Z — fztu session closes; post-session activity unknown (chunk 20)
2015-12-10T10:04:52Z — 60.2.12.12 hammers root 5 times with port cycling in ~30 seconds (chunk 20)
2015-12-10T10:59:45Z — 183.62.140.253 begins sustained root brute-force: 60+ attempts at 2s cadence over ~2.75 minutes (chunk 23, CORR-07)
2015-12-10T11:00:57Z — Second attacker 88.147.143.242 probes invalid user 'sandeep' concurrently with 183.62.140.253 campaign (chunk 23)
2015-12-10T11:02:28Z — Approximate end of 183.62.140.253 brute-force burst (chunk 23)
recommended_next_queries: 1. VALIDATE H2 (fztu compromise): Query auth.log and secure log for all activity by user 'fztu' and UID=0 sessions from 119.137.62.142 — specifically: commands executed during the 09:32–09:45 session, any files created/modified, and whether 119.137.62.142 appears in any prior successful or failed auth events.

2. VALIDATE H1 (botnet campaign): Cross-reference all attacking IPs from chunks 14, 20, and 23 against threat intelligence feeds (AbuseIPDB, Shodan, VirusTotal) to determine if they share ASN, registration, or known botnet attribution. Also query firewall/iptables logs for block/allow decisions on these IPs.

3. VALIDATE H3 (mod_jk misconfiguration): Inspect /etc/httpd/conf/workers2.properties and Tomcat connector configuration on the 2005 host. Determine if Tomcat was running during the 13:53–19:15Z window and whether the backend was reachable. Query Tomcat logs for corresponding connector errors.

4. VALIDATE H4 (logrotate suppression): Run 'logrotate --debug /etc/logrotate.conf' on the 2015 host to identify which log file or post-rotate script is causing exit code 1. Check disk usage (df -h) and inode counts to rule out disk exhaustion as a contributing factor. Verify /var/log/secure and /var/log/messages are rotating correctly.

5. ENUMERATE ATTACK SURFACE: Query all chunks for any successful authentication events (not just fztu) — specifically 'Accepted password', 'Accepted publickey', 'session opened' — to identify any other potentially compromised accounts during the 2015 brute-force campaign.

6. CORRELATE FTP FLOODS (chunks 14, 16, 17): Extract all FTP source IPs and connection timestamps from chunks 11, 14, 16, and 17. Determine if FTP flood IPs overlap with SSH brute-force IPs (CORR-01 validation). Query vsftpd/ftpd logs for any successful FTP logins during the flood windows.

7. BASELINE DEVIATION CHECK (chunks 25–32): Identify the exact process names and system configuration changes driving the consistent ~45 process_lifecycle and ~8–12 system_configuration counts per chunk. Confirm these are cron-driven (e.g., crond, anacron) and not attacker-planted persistence mechanisms masquerading as scheduled jobs.

8. DNS ACTIVITY (chunk 17): Chunk 17 contains 6 dns_service_discovery events — the only such events in the dataset. Query DNS/named logs for the corresponding timestamps to determine if these represent legitimate service discovery, DNS reconnaissance, or C2 beacon activity coinciding with the authz_security spike (195 events) in the same chunk.

9. TEMPORAL GAP ANALYSIS: Chunks 3–7 and 9–13 contain mostly zero-count placeholders. Determine whether these represent truly empty log segments or parsing/ingestion failures. Missing log data during an active attack campaign (2015 window) would be a significant blind spot.

10. ACCOUNT AUDIT: For the 2015 host, run 'last', 'lastb', and 'aureport --auth' to produce a complete authentication history. Cross-reference against the attacking IPs and the fztu session to determine the full scope of any successful intrusions.

chunk_overview.summary: Chunk 8 covers Apache/mod_jk activity on 2005-12-05 from ~13:53Z to ~19:15Z. The dominant pattern is a persistent, recurring cycle: Apache spawns new child worker processes (jk2_init, E1), workerEnv initializes (E2), and then immediately enters an error state (E3 — "mod_jk child workerEnv in error state"). This cycle repeats dozens of times across the entire observation window, indicating a systemic instability in the mod_jk/Tomcat connector configuration. Five separate "Directory index forbidden" errors (E4) from distinct external IPs suggest repeated directory-listing probes against /var/www/html/, which is a low-level reconnaissance pattern worth noting.

This chunk spans 2015-07-11 through 2015-07-17 and is dominated by two major threat patterns: (1) repeated SSH brute-force attempts against the root account from multiple external IPs (211.137.205.253, 68.143.156.89, 202-132-40-29, c51471f2c.cable.wanadoo.nl, 61-220-159-99.hinet-ip.hinet.net), and (2) a massive FTP connection flood from at least six distinct external IPs (211.107.232.1, 212.65.68.82, 83.116.207.11, 218.146.61.230, 207.30.238.8, 203.101.45.59, 210.245.165.136), with dozens of connections per source in tight time windows suggesting automated scanning or brute-force tooling. Routine daily logrotate jobs consistently exit abnormally (exit code 1), and scheduled su sessions for system accounts (cyrus, news) occur each morning around 04:00 UTC. A suspicious SSH session for the generic 'test' account was opened and closed three times in rapid succession on 2015-07-13. A one-time cupsd restart and syslogd restart occurred on 2015-07-17 during the logrotate window.

This chunk is dominated by a sustained, multi-source SSH brute-force campaign spanning 09:17–10:14 UTC on 2015-12-10. The primary attacker (187.141.143.180, customer-187-141-143-180-sta.uninet-ide.com.mx) systematically cycles through well-known service/default usernames (postgres, nagios, www, abc, ted, vnc, git, ghost, ubuntu, ftp, test, deploy, oralce, oracle, nagios1, postgres1, test1, test2, bssh, mysql, magnos, ingrid, jay, cyrus) at ~5-second intervals, with repeated DNS reverse-mapping failures flagged as POSSIBLE BREAK-IN ATTEMPT. A second attacker (103.207.39.16) joins mid-chunk targeting support, uucp, admin. A third source (104.192.3.34) attempts invalid user FILTER and root. A fourth (60.2.12.12) hammers the root account 5 times in rapid succession (~10:04–10:05). A fifth (119.4.203.64) retries invalid user admin 6 times on the same port (2191) in under 15 seconds. A sixth (52.80.34.196, AWS China) targets matlab. One legitimate successful login is observed: user fztu from 119.137.62.142 at 09:32:20, session closed at 09:45:06. One incomplete connection probe is noted from 181.214.87.4 (no identification string, failed none auth). All events originate from openssh/sshd.

This chunk is dominated by a sustained, high-rate SSH brute-force attack against the root account originating from 183.62.140.253, spanning 10:59:45Z to 11:02:28Z (roughly 2.75 minutes). Every ~2 seconds a new connection is attempted, fails authentication, and immediately disconnects — a classic automated credential-stuffing pattern. A secondary attacker from 88.147.143.242 attempted to authenticate as the non-existent user "sandeep" at 11:00:57Z–11:00:59Z. All events are sourced from openssh/sshd and fall squarely into the authz_security category. No successful logins are recorded.

key_correlations.explanation: In chunk 20 (2015-12-10, 09:17–10:14 UTC) and chunk 23 (2015-12-10, 10:59–11:02 UTC), at least 8 distinct source IPs are attacking SSH simultaneously or in rapid succession. The diversity of source IPs, attack styles (username cycling, root hammering, port cycling, scanner probes), and tight temporal clustering strongly suggests botnet-coordinated activity rather than independent opportunistic scanning.

hypotheses: H1 — Coordinated SSH+FTP credential-stuffing campaign (2015): The simultaneous appearance of high-rate SSH brute-force (chunks 14, 17, 18, 20, 22, 23) and FTP connection floods (chunks 14, 16, 17) from geographically diverse IPs across the same multi-day window suggests a single coordinated campaign or shared attack toolkit/botnet targeting both SSH (port 22) and FTP (port 21) on the same host.

H2 — Successful SSH compromise via brute-force (chunk 20): The accepted password login for user 'fztu' (uid=0) from 119.137.62.142 at 09:32:20 on 2015-12-10, occurring 15 minutes into an active multi-source brute-force campaign, may represent a successful intrusion. The 13-minute interactive session and root-equivalent UID strongly suggest post-exploitation activity.

H3 — Apache/mod_jk misconfiguration enabling reconnaissance (2005, chunks 1–2, 7–8): The persistent mod_jk workerEnv error cycle (60+ occurrences) degraded the web application stack, and the concurrent directory-listing probes from 5 distinct IPs may indicate that attackers detected the degraded state and escalated enumeration. The instability may have been triggered or exploited by the reconnaissance activity.

H4 — Logrotate misconfiguration causing service disruption (2015, chunk 14): Daily logrotate exit-code-1 failures across the entire 2015-07 window, combined with a cupsd+syslogd restart on 2015-07-17 during the logrotate window, suggest a broken post-rotate script that is intermittently disrupting services. This could suppress log evidence of attacks if log files are not rotating correctly.

H5 — Chunks 25–32 represent a stable operational baseline: The highly consistent process_lifecycle (~45/chunk) and system_configuration (~8–12/chunk) counts across 8 consecutive chunks with near-zero security events suggest a scheduled-job or service-heartbeat baseline. Any future deviation from these counts in these categories should be treated as an anomaly indicator.

H6 — Multi-epoch log aggregation: The presence of events from both 2005 and 2015 in the same dataset suggests logs from multiple systems or time periods have been aggregated. Correlations across epochs should be treated as independent incident threads unless a shared infrastructure link is confirmed.

next_queries: 1. VALIDATE H2 (fztu compromise): Query auth.log and secure log for all activity by user 'fztu' and UID=0 sessions from 119.137.62.142 — specifically: commands executed during the 09:32–09:45 session, any files created/modified, and whether 119.137.62.142 appears in any prior successful or failed auth events.

2. VALIDATE H1 (botnet campaign): Cross-reference all attacking IPs from chunks 14, 20, and 23 against threat intelligence feeds (AbuseIPDB, Shodan, VirusTotal) to determine if they share ASN, registration, or known botnet attribution. Also query firewall/iptables logs for block/allow decisions on these IPs.

3. VALIDATE H3 (mod_jk misconfiguration): Inspect /etc/httpd/conf/workers2.properties and Tomcat connector configuration on the 2005 host. Determine if Tomcat was running during the 13:53–19:15Z window and whether the backend was reachable. Query Tomcat logs for corresponding connector errors.

4. VALIDATE H4 (logrotate suppression): Run 'logrotate --debug /etc/logrotate.conf' on the 2015 host to identify which log file or post-rotate script is causing exit code 1. Check disk usage (df -h) and inode counts to rule out disk exhaustion as a contributing factor. Verify /var/log/secure and /var/log/messages are rotating correctly.

5. ENUMERATE ATTACK SURFACE: Query all chunks for any successful authentication events (not just fztu) — specifically 'Accepted password', 'Accepted publickey', 'session opened' — to identify any other potentially compromised accounts during the 2015 brute-force campaign.

6. CORRELATE FTP FLOODS (chunks 14, 16, 17): Extract all FTP source IPs and connection timestamps from chunks 11, 14, 16, and 17. Determine if FTP flood IPs overlap with SSH brute-force IPs (CORR-01 validation). Query vsftpd/ftpd logs for any successful FTP logins during the flood windows.

7. BASELINE DEVIATION CHECK (chunks 25–32): Identify the exact process names and system configuration changes driving the consistent ~45 process_lifecycle and ~8–12 system_configuration counts per chunk. Confirm these are cron-driven (e.g., crond, anacron) and not attacker-planted persistence mechanisms masquerading as scheduled jobs.

8. DNS ACTIVITY (chunk 17): Chunk 17 contains 6 dns_service_discovery events — the only such events in the dataset. Query DNS/named logs for the corresponding timestamps to determine if these represent legitimate service discovery, DNS reconnaissance, or C2 beacon activity coinciding with the authz_security spike (195 events) in the same chunk.

9. TEMPORAL GAP ANALYSIS: Chunks 3–7 and 9–13 contain mostly zero-count placeholders. Determine whether these represent truly empty log segments or parsing/ingestion failures. Missing log data during an active attack campaign (2015 window) would be a significant blind spot.

10. ACCOUNT AUDIT: For the 2015 host, run 'last', 'lastb', and 'aureport --auth' to produce a complete authentication history. Cross-reference against the attacking IPs and the fztu session to determine the full scope of any successful intrusions.

evidence: E3 'mod_jk child workerEnv in error state' fires repeatedly from 13:53Z through 19:15Z — over 60 occurrences across the chunk
E2 'workerEnv.init() ok /etc/httpd/conf/workers2.properties' always immediately precedes an E3 error, confirming init succeeds but runtime connector fails
Worker PIDs continuously increment (6052→6751+), showing Apache is repeatedly spawning new children that all fail the same way
E1 jk2_init() logs show continuous child process spawning across the entire window (PIDs 6052–6791)
Scoreboard slots 6–10 are reused repeatedly, indicating rapid child turnover consistent with crash-restart cycling
Five distinct external IPs (141.154.18.244, 216.216.185.130, 218.75.106.250, 68.228.3.15, 61.220.139.68) each triggered 'Directory index forbidden by rule: /var/www/html/' across a ~5-hour window
All five probes target the same path (/var/www/html/), consistent with distributed web root enumeration/reconnaissance

suspicious_events.rationale: mod_jk child workerEnv enters error state immediately at the start of the log window (13:53Z) and continues recurring throughout the entire ~5.5-hour window. The error persists across dozens of worker respawn cycles, indicating a persistent misconfiguration or backend (Tomcat) connectivity failure in workers2.properties.
Client 141.154.18.244 attempted to access /var/www/html/ directory index, which is forbidden. This is a potential directory enumeration/reconnaissance probe.
Client 216.216.185.130 attempted to access /var/www/html/ directory index. Second distinct external IP probing the web root within ~2.5 hours of the first.
Client 218.75.106.250 attempted to access /var/www/html/ directory index. Third distinct external IP — pattern of distributed directory probing.
Client 68.228.3.15 attempted to access /var/www/html/ directory index. Fourth distinct IP probing the web root.

Client 61.220.139.68 attempted to access /var/www/html/ directory index. Fifth distinct IP — five different source IPs probing the same forbidden path across ~5 hours is a notable reconnaissance pattern.

chunk_analyses.summary: Chunk 8 covers Apache/mod_jk activity on 2005-12-05 from ~13:53Z to ~19:15Z. The dominant pattern is a persistent, recurring cycle: Apache spawns new child worker processes (jk2_init, E1), workerEnv initializes (E2), and then immediately enters an error state (E3 — "mod_jk child workerEnv in error state"). This cycle repeats dozens of times across the entire observation window, indicating a systemic instability in the mod_jk/Tomcat connector configuration. Five separate "Directory index forbidden" errors (E4) from distinct external IPs suggest repeated directory-listing probes against /var/www/html/, which is a low-level reconnaissance pattern worth noting.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk spans 2015-07-11 through 2015-07-17 and is dominated by two major threat patterns: (1) repeated SSH brute-force attempts against the root account from multiple external IPs (211.137.205.253, 68.143.156.89, 202-132-40-29, c51471f2c.cable.wanadoo.nl, 61-220-159-99.hinet-ip.hinet.net), and (2) a massive FTP connection flood from at least six distinct external IPs (211.107.232.1, 212.65.68.82, 83.116.207.11, 218.146.61.230, 207.30.238.8, 203.101.45.59, 210.245.165.136), with dozens of connections per source in tight time windows suggesting automated scanning or brute-force tooling. Routine daily logrotate jobs consistently exit abnormally (exit code 1), and scheduled su sessions for system accounts (cyrus, news) occur each morning around 04:00 UTC. A suspicious SSH session for the generic 'test' account was opened and closed three times in rapid succession on 2015-07-13. A one-time cupsd restart and syslogd restart occurred on 2015-07-17 during the logrotate window.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk is dominated by a sustained, multi-source SSH brute-force campaign spanning 09:17–10:14 UTC on 2015-12-10. The primary attacker (187.141.143.180, customer-187-141-143-180-sta.uninet-ide.com.mx) systematically cycles through well-known service/default usernames (postgres, nagios, www, abc, ted, vnc, git, ghost, ubuntu, ftp, test, deploy, oralce, oracle, nagios1, postgres1, test1, test2, bssh, mysql, magnos, ingrid, jay, cyrus) at ~5-second intervals, with repeated DNS reverse-mapping failures flagged as POSSIBLE BREAK-IN ATTEMPT. A second attacker (103.207.39.16) joins mid-chunk targeting support, uucp, admin. A third source (104.192.3.34) attempts invalid user FILTER and root. A fourth (60.2.12.12) hammers the root account 5 times in rapid succession (~10:04–10:05). A fifth (119.4.203.64) retries invalid user admin 6 times on the same port (2191) in under 15 seconds. A sixth (52.80.34.196, AWS China) targets matlab. One legitimate successful login is observed: user fztu from 119.137.62.142 at 09:32:20, session closed at 09:45:06. One incomplete connection probe is noted from 181.214.87.4 (no identification string, failed none auth). All events originate from openssh/sshd.

Connection closed by 88.147.143.242 [preauth] — attacker disconnected immediately after failed attempt, consistent with automated tooling.

chunk_analyses.summary: This chunk is dominated by a sustained, high-rate SSH brute-force attack against the root account originating from 183.62.140.253, spanning 10:59:45Z to 11:02:28Z (roughly 2.75 minutes). Every ~2 seconds a new connection is attempted, fails authentication, and immediately disconnects — a classic automated credential-stuffing pattern. A secondary attacker from 88.147.143.242 attempted to authenticate as the non-existent user "sandeep" at 11:00:57Z–11:00:59Z. All events are sourced from openssh/sshd and fall squarely into the authz_security category. No successful logins are recorded.
